news_summary: Today's health news highlights promising scientific advances, including a breakthrough cholesterol treatment without statins, easier ways to build muscle, and the discovery of rare medicinal compounds in cannabis leaves.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité santé d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées scientifiques prometteuses, notamment un traitement révolutionnaire contre le cholestérol sans statines, des méthodes plus simples pour développer sa masse musculaire et la découverte de composés médicinaux rares dans les feuilles de cannabis.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de salud de hoy destacan avances científicos prometedores, entre los que se incluyen un tratamiento revolucionario contra el colesterol sin estatinas, formas más sencillas de desarrollar masa muscular y el descubrimiento de compuestos medicinales poco comunes en las hojas de cannabis.

articles.title: The news is not all bad: five inspiring science stories to lift your mood
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 01 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01290-5Species recovery, cancer-preventing vaccines and progress in developing renewable-energy sources are just some of the positive developments that have happened this year so far.
articles.content:
This newly identified species of glass frog (provisional name Nymphargus dajomesae) was found in the Cordillera del Cóndor mountain range in the Andes.Credit: Photo archive PUCE-BIOWEB

It is easy to feel overwhelmed by the state of the world from reading the news.

For that reason, in December last year, Nature gathered seven good news stories of 2025 that offered optimism for the future. Readers devoured these hopeful tales.

In this latest round of positive scientific developments that you might have missed, you’ll learn about the discovery of new species, a promising medical treatment for a fatal mitochondria disease and a biofuel made from date palm trees.

Life-saving treatment

In March, the World Health Organization has approved the use of the first-ever malaria treatment for babies and infants. The drug, called artemether-lumefantrine, is the specifically formulated for infants weighing between two and five kilograms and can now be bought and distributed by the United Nations, non-governmental organizations and other agencies.

In 2024, 610,000 people died from malaria, mostly in Africa, with children under five accounting for about three quarters of the deaths in the region.

Until now, babies and infants have been treated with medication made for children weighing at least five kilograms, meaning that doctors had to break up tablets and estimate the correct amount to administer. This sometimes led to children receiving too much or too little of the drug components, which can be harmful or make treatment ineffective.

Repurposed drug

Six people with a previously untreatable disorder affecting their mitochondrial function have been successfully treated with the drug sildenafil1, sold as Viagra. Leigh syndrome is a severe genetic condition that affects one in 40,000 newborns. Symptoms usually start during infancy or early childhood, and can include developmental delay or regression, muscle weakness, breathing problems or eye conditions. Many children with the condition die from sudden respiratory failure before they turn three.

Scientists in the United States and Europe used stem cells taken from people with Leigh syndrome to test 5,600 existing drugs, identifying Viagra as a potential option. They treated six people who had Leigh syndrome whose ages ranged from nine months to 38 years old. Most saw improvements in their mobility, muscle strength and ability to breathe independently. The treatment still needs to be tested in more people and against a placebo.

Energy from unlikely places

To reduce the need for fossil fuels, alternative sources of energy are in high demand.

A UK team has used bacteria to generate the hydrogen gas2 that is needed to produce drugs, fuels, preserved foods and plastics. Currently, hydrogen is obtained through a process called steam reforming, which releases large amounts of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. The new process involves growing a strain of Escherichia coli that naturally produces hydrogen when deprived of oxygen. The researchers added a palladium catalyst and substrate for the hydrogen to bind to, and when they removed oxygen, hydrogen was bound to 94% of the substrate.

The team then turned waste bread into a food source that could be given to the bacteria instead of glucose, to show that this type of food waste can be repurposed. The system resulted in a three-fold decrease in greenhouse-gas equivalent emissions compared with using fossil fuels, according to the team’s modelling.

Another research group has developed a way to extract biofuel from dry surface fibres growing in the leaf crown at the top of date palm trees (Phoenix dactylifera)3.

The team heated up the fibres and found that they contained three major classes of hydrocarbon molecule that are used in biofuels. The researchers say their work could also help to avoid the need for expensive waste-management systems to dispose of surface fibres in regions that have a lot of date-palm waste. The authors’ process is an alternative to the current practice of burning the material, which releases pollutants and can cause breathing issues for people exposed to the smoke.

Cancer progress

The benefits of vaccinating young women against human papillomavirus (HPV), which can cause cancers of the genitals, head and neck, have been known for at least a decade. Data from the United States published last month now reveal the positive impact of vaccinating men against the virus4.

Researchers compared the rates of cancer among 510,000 boys and men aged between 9 and 26 years old who were vaccinated against HPV, and another 510,000 who were unvaccinated. They found that vaccination was linked to a 46% reduction in the risk of developing cancers of the oesophagus, head, neck, penis and anal tissue. The HPV vaccine is “an absolute winner” for cancer prevention, says Jarad Martin, a radiation oncologist at private health-care provider GenesisCare in Newcastle, Australia.
articles.reasoning: The article reports multiple significant scientific and medical breakthroughs with broad positive impacts, including a new malaria treatment for infants, promising treatment for Leigh syndrome, advances in renewable biofuels, and evidence of HPV vaccination reducing cancer risk. These developments have tangible benefits for global health and the environment, are well-detailed, and affect large populations.
articles.personality_title: New medical and environmental breakthroughs offer hope worldwide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people feel worried by negative news, but recent scientific discoveries show progress in health and energy. These advances include new treatments for diseases and cleaner ways to produce fuel.

**What happened** – In 2026, several important developments were announced: a new malaria medicine designed for babies was approved by the World Health Organization; a drug originally made for erectile dysfunction helped patients with a rare genetic disease called Leigh syndrome; scientists found ways to create hydrogen fuel using bacteria and to make biofuel from date palm tree fibers; and studies showed that vaccinating boys against HPV lowers their risk of several cancers.

**Impact** – These discoveries matter because they improve health and protect the environment. The malaria drug means babies can get the right dose safely, potentially saving many lives in Africa. The repurposed drug offers hope for people with a disease that had no treatment. Using bacteria and plant waste for fuel can reduce pollution and dependence on fossil fuels. The HPV vaccine study shows that vaccination helps both girls and boys avoid cancer.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue testing the new Leigh syndrome treatment with more patients to confirm its benefits. The biofuel and hydrogen methods will be further developed for larger-scale use. Health organizations may expand vaccine programs for boys to reduce cancer risks globally.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New medicines and clean energy discoveries in 2026 are helping protect people’s health and the planet in practical ways.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelles avancées médicales et environnementales offrent de l'espoir dans le monde entier
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes s'inquiètent des mauvaises nouvelles, mais des découvertes scientifiques récentes montrent des progrès en santé et en énergie. Ces avancées comprennent de nouveaux traitements pour des maladies et des méthodes plus propres pour produire du carburant.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En 2026, plusieurs développements importants ont été annoncés : un nouveau médicament contre le paludisme conçu pour les bébés a été approuvé par l'Organisation mondiale de la santé ; un médicament initialement destiné à la dysfonction érectile a aidé des patients atteints d'une maladie génétique rare appelée syndrome de Leigh ; des scientifiques ont trouvé des moyens de produire de l'hydrogène à partir de bactéries et de fabriquer du biocarburant à partir des fibres des palmiers dattiers ; et des études ont montré que vacciner les garçons contre le HPV réduit leur risque de plusieurs cancers.

**Impact** – Ces découvertes sont importantes car elles améliorent la santé et protègent l'environnement. Le médicament contre le paludisme permet aux bébés de recevoir une dose correcte en toute sécurité, ce qui pourrait sauver de nombreuses vies en Afrique. Le médicament réutilisé offre de l'espoir aux personnes atteintes d'une maladie sans traitement. Utiliser des bactéries et des déchets végétaux pour le carburant peut réduire la pollution et la dépendance aux combustibles fossiles. L'étude sur le vaccin contre le HPV montre que la vaccination aide filles et garçons à éviter le cancer.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à tester le nouveau traitement du syndrome de Leigh sur plus de patients pour confirmer ses bienfaits. Les méthodes de production de biocarburant et d'hydrogène seront développées à plus grande échelle. Les organisations de santé pourraient élargir les programmes de vaccination des garçons pour réduire les risques de cancer dans le monde.

**Phrase clé** – De nouveaux médicaments et découvertes en énergie propre en 2026 aident concrètement à protéger la santé des personnes et la planète.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos avances médicos y ambientales ofrecen esperanza en todo el mundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas se preocupan por las malas noticias, pero descubrimientos científicos recientes muestran avances en salud y energía. Estos incluyen nuevos tratamientos para enfermedades y formas más limpias de producir combustible.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026 se anunciaron varios desarrollos importantes: un nuevo medicamento para la malaria diseñado para bebés fue aprobado por la Organización Mundial de la Salud; un medicamento originalmente para la disfunción eréctil ayudó a pacientes con una enfermedad genética rara llamada síndrome de Leigh; científicos encontraron maneras de producir hidrógeno con bacterias y fabricar biocombustible a partir de fibras de palmeras datileras; y estudios mostraron que vacunar a los niños contra el VPH reduce su riesgo de varios tipos de cáncer.

**Impacto** – Estos descubrimientos son importantes porque mejoran la salud y protegen el medio ambiente. El medicamento contra la malaria permite que los bebés reciban la dosis correcta de forma segura, lo que podría salvar muchas vidas en África. El medicamento reutilizado ofrece esperanza para personas con una enfermedad sin tratamiento. Usar bacterias y desechos vegetales para combustible puede reducir la contaminación y la dependencia de combustibles fósiles. El estudio sobre la vacuna contra el VPH muestra que la vacunación ayuda tanto a niñas como a niños a evitar el cáncer.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán probando el nuevo tratamiento para el síndrome de Leigh en más pacientes para confirmar sus beneficios. Los métodos para producir biocombustible e hidrógeno se desarrollarán a mayor escala. Las organizaciones de salud podrían ampliar los programas de vacunación para niños y así reducir los riesgos de cáncer en todo el mundo.

**Frase clave** – Nuevos medicamentos y descubrimientos en energía limpia en 2026 están ayudando a proteger la salud de las personas y el planeta de manera práctica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a translucent glass frog perched gently on a vibrant green leaf in a misty Andean mountain setting, beside a cluster of delicate date palm fibers glowing softly with golden hues, and a subtle, stylized cluster of microscopic cells transforming into shining hydrogen molecules, all rendered in soft natural tones and gentle light.

articles.title: New treatment cuts bad cholesterol by nearly 50% without statins
articles.summary: A new breakthrough could change how high cholesterol is treated, offering a powerful alternative to traditional drugs. Researchers have developed tiny DNA-based molecules that shut down PCSK9—a key protein that keeps “bad” LDL cholesterol circulating in the blood. By blocking this protein, cells can absorb more cholesterol instead of letting it build up in arteries, dramatically lowering levels linked to heart disease.
articles.content:
High levels of cholesterol in the bloodstream can lead to hypercholesterolemia, a condition that damages arteries and raises the risk of heart disease. Now, researchers from the University of Barcelona and the University of Oregon have developed a promising new approach to control cholesterol levels, offering a potential new way to combat atherosclerosis, which occurs when fatty plaques build up in artery walls.

The research team focused on PCSK9, a protein that plays a central role in regulating levels of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), often called "bad" cholesterol. They designed a method to block the production of this protein using specialized DNA-based molecules known as polypurine hairpins (PPRH). By suppressing PCSK9, the treatment helps cells absorb more cholesterol, reducing the amount circulating in the blood and limiting buildup in the arteries. Importantly, this approach may avoid the side effects commonly linked to statin medications.

The findings were published in the journal Biochemical Pharmacology. The study was led by Carles J. Ciudad and Verònica Noé from the University of Barcelona's Faculty of Pharmacy and Food Sciences and the Institute of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (IN2UB), working with Nathalie Pamir at the University of Oregon in Portland (United States). Funding came from the Spanish Ministry of Science, Innovation and Universities (MICINN) and the National Institutes of Health (NIH) in the United States.

How Polypurine Hairpins Block a Key Cholesterol Protein

PCSK9 (protein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9) has become an important target in recent years for therapies aimed at lowering cholesterol and reducing cardiovascular risk. The protein works by attaching to LDL receptors on cells, limiting their ability to remove cholesterol from the bloodstream. When PCSK9 levels are high, fewer receptors are available, causing LDL cholesterol to accumulate in the blood.

Polypurine hairpins (PPRHs) offer a way to interrupt this process at the genetic level. These molecules are short strands of DNA that can bind very precisely to specific DNA or RNA sequences and block gene activity. In this case, PPRHs prevent the PCSK9 gene from being transcribed, which leads to an increase in LDLR receptor levels and improves the body's ability to pull cholesterol out of circulation. As a result, overall cholesterol levels drop and the risk of plaque formation is reduced.

The study describes for the first time how two specific PPRHs, called HpE9 and HpE12, reduce both PCSK9 RNA and protein while boosting LDL receptor levels.

"Specifically, one of the arms of each chain of the HpE9 and HpE12 polypurines binds specifically to polypyrimidine sequences of exons 9 and 12 of PCSK9, respectively, via Watson-Crick bonds," notes Professor Carles J. Ciudad, from the Department of Biochemistry and Physiology. This interaction blocks gene transcription and interferes with the activity of RNA polymerase or transcription factors.

Strong Results in Cells and Animal Models

The researchers tested the therapy in laboratory-grown liver cells and in transgenic mice that carry the human PCSK9 gene. The results were significant.

"The results show that both HpE9 and HpE12 are highly effective in HepG2 cells. HpE12 decreases PCSK9 RNA levels by 74% and protein levels by 87%. In the case of transgenic mice, a single injection of HpE12 reduces plasma PCSK9 levels by 50% and cholesterol levels by 47% on the third day," says Professor Verònica Noé.

A Potential Alternative to Statins

Since PCSK9 has become a major target for cholesterol-lowering therapies, several approaches have been developed to block its effects. These include gene-silencing technologies such as siRNAs, antisense oligonucleotides, and CRISPR-based methods. Existing treatments like Inclisiran, an siRNA therapy, and monoclonal antibodies such as evolocumab and alirocumab are already in use.

However, PPRHs may offer unique advantages. "PPRHs, especially HpE12, are therapeutic oligonucleotides with many advantages, including low cost of synthesis, stability and lack of immunogenicity. In addition, such a PPRH-based approach against PCSK9 would not lead to side effects such as the myopathies associated with statin therapy," the experts conclude.

If confirmed in further studies, this new strategy could provide a safer and more targeted way to lower cholesterol and reduce the risk of heart disease.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough in cholesterol treatment using DNA-based molecules to block PCSK9, offering a potentially safer and more effective alternative to statins. The research has broad implications for reducing heart disease risk, a major global health issue, and is supported by detailed experimental results in cells and animal models. This represents a meaningful advancement with potential large-scale health benefits.
articles.personality_title: New DNA-based treatment cuts bad cholesterol by nearly half without statins
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – High cholesterol can cause heart disease by clogging arteries. Usually, people use drugs called statins to lower bad cholesterol, but these can have side effects.

**What happened** – Scientists from the University of Barcelona and University of Oregon created tiny DNA molecules called polypurine hairpins (PPRHs) that block a protein named PCSK9. PCSK9 normally stops cells from removing bad cholesterol from the blood. By blocking PCSK9, cells can absorb more cholesterol, lowering its level in the blood. The researchers tested this in liver cells and mice, finding cholesterol dropped by nearly 50% after treatment.

**Impact** – This new method lowers bad cholesterol without using statins, which often cause muscle pain and other problems. The DNA molecules are stable, low-cost, and don’t trigger immune reactions. This could give doctors a safer option to reduce heart disease risk.

**What's next step** – Researchers need to test this treatment in human trials to confirm its safety and effectiveness. If successful, it might become a new medicine for people who cannot tolerate statins or need stronger cholesterol control.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new DNA-based therapy blocks PCSK9 to lower bad cholesterol by almost half, offering a promising alternative to statins with fewer side effects.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau traitement à base d’ADN réduit le mauvais cholestérol de près de moitié sans statines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Un taux élevé de cholestérol peut boucher les artères et provoquer des maladies cardiaques. Habituellement, on utilise des statines pour réduire ce cholestérol, mais elles peuvent avoir des effets secondaires.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs des universités de Barcelone et de l’Oregon ont créé de petites molécules d’ADN appelées polypurines en épingle à cheveux (PPRH) qui bloquent une protéine nommée PCSK9. PCSK9 empêche normalement les cellules d’éliminer le mauvais cholestérol du sang. En bloquant cette protéine, les cellules absorbent plus de cholestérol, réduisant ainsi son niveau dans le sang. Les tests en laboratoire et sur des souris ont montré une baisse du cholestérol de près de 50%.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode réduit le mauvais cholestérol sans utiliser de statines, qui provoquent souvent des douleurs musculaires et d’autres problèmes. Les molécules d’ADN sont stables, peu coûteuses et ne déclenchent pas de réaction immunitaire. Cela pourrait offrir aux médecins une option plus sûre pour prévenir les maladies cardiaques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs doivent tester ce traitement sur des humains pour confirmer son efficacité et sa sécurité. S’il réussit, il pourrait devenir un nouveau médicament pour ceux qui ne tolèrent pas les statines ou qui ont besoin d’un contrôle plus fort du cholestérol.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle thérapie à base d’ADN bloque PCSK9 pour réduire le mauvais cholestérol de presque moitié, offrant une alternative prometteuse aux statines avec moins d’effets secondaires.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo tratamiento con ADN reduce el colesterol malo casi a la mitad sin estatinas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El colesterol alto puede tapar las arterias y causar enfermedades del corazón. Normalmente, se usan medicamentos llamados estatinas para bajarlo, pero pueden causar efectos secundarios.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Barcelona y la Universidad de Oregón crearon pequeñas moléculas de ADN llamadas horquillas polipurinas (PPRH) que bloquean una proteína llamada PCSK9. PCSK9 impide que las células eliminen el colesterol malo de la sangre. Al bloquear esta proteína, las células absorben más colesterol, reduciendo su nivel en la sangre. En pruebas con células hepáticas y ratones, el colesterol bajó casi un 50%.

**Impacto** – Este método nuevo reduce el colesterol malo sin usar estatinas, que a menudo causan dolor muscular y otros problemas. Las moléculas de ADN son estables, económicas y no provocan reacciones inmunes. Esto podría dar a los médicos una opción más segura para reducir el riesgo de enfermedades cardíacas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores deben probar este tratamiento en humanos para confirmar que es seguro y eficaz. Si funciona, podría ser un nuevo medicamento para quienes no toleran las estatinas o necesitan controlar mejor su colesterol.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una nueva terapia con ADN bloquea PCSK9 para bajar el colesterol malo casi a la mitad, ofreciendo una alternativa prometedora a las estatinas con menos efectos secundarios.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a stylized human liver shaped like a glowing shield, with delicate, intertwined DNA strands gently wrapping around and calming a cluster of small, dark, spiky protein shapes labeled by their form as PCSK9, while numerous bright, open receptor-like gates on the liver surface allow smooth flow of golden cholesterol particles away from the shield, symbolizing blocked harmful proteins and lowered bad cholesterol without harsh chemicals.

articles.title: You don’t need intense workouts to build muscle, new study reveals
articles.summary: Building muscle doesn’t have to mean exhausting workouts or soreness. Researchers found that slow, controlled “lowering” movements can boost strength more efficiently while requiring less effort. Even five minutes a day of simple exercises like chair squats or wall push-ups can make a real difference. It’s a smarter, easier way to get stronger—no gym required.
articles.content:
If you believe getting stronger requires pushing yourself to the limit at the gym, new research suggests otherwise. Findings from Edith Cowan University (ECU) show that improving muscle size, strength, and performance does not depend on exhausting workouts or feeling sore afterward.

"The idea that exercise must be exhausting or painful is holding people back," ECU's Director of Exercise and Sports Science, Professor Ken Nosaka, said.

He points to a different approach that can be more effective and far easier to stick with. "Instead, we should be focusing on eccentric exercises which can deliver stronger results with far less effort than traditional exercise -- and you don't even need a gym!"

What Is Eccentric Exercise

Eccentric exercise focuses on the phase when muscles lengthen rather than shorten. This typically happens during the lowering portion of a movement, such as bringing a dumbbell down, walking downstairs, or slowly lowering yourself into a chair.

According to the study, muscles can produce greater force during these lengthening movements while using less energy than they would during lifting, pulling, or climbing actions.

More Strength With Less Effort

"You can gain strength without feeling as exhausted. So, you get more benefit for less effort. That makes eccentric exercise appealing for a wide range of people," Professor Nosaka said.

Although these movements can sometimes lead to mild soreness, especially for beginners, discomfort is not required to see progress.

Simple Exercises You Can Do At Home

Eccentric exercises are easy to incorporate into daily routines and do not require special equipment. Examples include chair squats, heel drops, and wall push-ups. Research shows that just five minutes a day of these movements can lead to meaningful improvements in strength and overall health.

Ideal For Older Adults And Beginners

Because eccentric exercise puts less strain on the heart and lungs, it is especially well suited for older adults and people with chronic health conditions. The movements also feel familiar, which makes them easier to adopt and maintain over time.

"These movements mirror what we already do in daily life. That makes them practical, realistic and easier to stick with," Professor Nosaka said.

"When exercise feels achievable, people keep doing it."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant research finding that a simpler, less strenuous form of exercise (eccentric exercise) can effectively build muscle and strength. This has broad positive implications for public health, especially for older adults and those with chronic conditions, by making strength training more accessible and sustainable. The article provides clear context, practical examples, and expert commentary, highlighting a meaningful advancement in exercise science with real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: Simple lowering movements build muscle without hard workouts, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people think building muscle means doing very hard workouts that leave you exhausted or sore. But new research from Edith Cowan University (ECU) shows this isn’t true. 

**What happened** – Scientists at ECU studied a type of exercise called eccentric exercise, which focuses on slowly lowering your body or weights. For example, slowly sitting down in a chair or lowering yourself during a push-up. They found that these movements help muscles get stronger while using less energy and effort. 

**Impact** – This is important because it means you can build muscle and strength without needing to push yourself to the limit or visit a gym. Just five minutes a day of simple exercises like chair squats or wall push-ups can improve your strength. It’s especially helpful for older people or those with health problems because it doesn’t strain the heart or lungs as much. 

**What’s next step** – Researchers hope more people will try eccentric exercises at home and include them in daily routines. Future studies might look at how these exercises help even more groups of people and how to spread this easy way to get stronger. 

**One-sentence takeaway** – Slowly lowering your body during simple exercises can build muscle and strength without hard workouts or special equipment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des mouvements simples de descente renforcent les muscles sans entraînements difficiles, révèle une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup pensent que pour développer ses muscles, il faut faire des entraînements très difficiles qui fatiguent ou rendent douloureux. Mais une nouvelle recherche de l’Université Edith Cowan (ECU) montre que ce n’est pas le cas.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les scientifiques d’ECU ont étudié un type d’exercice appelé exercice excentrique, qui consiste à descendre lentement son corps ou des poids. Par exemple, s’asseoir doucement sur une chaise ou descendre pendant une pompe. Ils ont découvert que ces mouvements renforcent les muscles tout en utilisant moins d’énergie et d’effort.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela signifie qu’on peut renforcer ses muscles sans se pousser à l’extrême ni aller à la salle de sport. Cinq minutes par jour d’exercices simples comme les squats sur chaise ou les pompes contre un mur peuvent améliorer la force. C’est particulièrement utile pour les personnes âgées ou avec des problèmes de santé car cela ne fatigue pas trop le cœur ou les poumons.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent que plus de personnes essaieront ces exercices à la maison et les intégreront dans leur routine quotidienne. Des études futures pourraient montrer comment ces exercices aident encore plus de personnes et comment les faire connaître largement.

**Phrase clé** – Descendre lentement son corps pendant des exercices simples peut renforcer les muscles sans entraînements difficiles ni équipement spécial.
articles.personality_title_es: Movimientos simples de bajada construyen músculo sin entrenamientos duros, revela estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas creen que para ganar músculo hay que hacer entrenamientos muy duros que dejan agotado o con dolor. Pero una nueva investigación de la Universidad Edith Cowan (ECU) muestra que no es así.

**Qué pasó** – Los científicos de ECU estudiaron un tipo de ejercicio llamado ejercicio excéntrico, que se centra en bajar lentamente el cuerpo o pesas. Por ejemplo, sentarse despacio en una silla o bajar durante una flexión. Descubrieron que estos movimientos fortalecen los músculos usando menos energía y esfuerzo.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque significa que puedes ganar músculo y fuerza sin tener que esforzarte mucho ni ir al gimnasio. Solo cinco minutos al día de ejercicios simples como sentadillas en silla o flexiones contra la pared pueden mejorar la fuerza. Es especialmente útil para personas mayores o con problemas de salud porque no exige tanto al corazón ni a los pulmones.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan que más personas prueben los ejercicios excéntricos en casa y los incluyan en su rutina diaria. Estudios futuros podrían explorar cómo estos ejercicios ayudan a más personas y cómo difundir esta forma fácil de fortalecerse.

**Frase clave** – Bajar lentamente el cuerpo durante ejercicios simples puede construir músculo y fuerza sin entrenamientos duros ni equipo especial.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy chair gently lowering itself with soft, flowing lines symbolizing controlled muscle lengthening, surrounded by subtle silhouettes of a descending staircase and a calm wall with hands pressing lightly against it, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones to evoke ease and steady strength without strain.

articles.title: Don’t toss cannabis leaves: Scientists found rare compounds with medical potential
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new layer of complexity in Cannabis, identifying dozens of previously unknown compounds—including the first-ever evidence of rare molecules called flavoalkaloids in its leaves. These compounds, prized for their potential health benefits, were hidden among a rich mix of plant chemicals that vary dramatically even between just a few strains.
articles.content:
Scientists at Stellenbosch University (SU) have uncovered the first evidence of a rare group of phenolic compounds known as flavoalkaloids in Cannabis leaves, adding a surprising new dimension to the plant's chemistry.

Phenolic compounds, particularly flavonoids, are already highly valued in medicine for their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-carcinogenic effects. This new finding suggests Cannabis may contain even more biologically important compounds than previously recognized.

Dozens of Previously Unknown Cannabis Compounds Identified

In their study, researchers analyzed three commercially grown Cannabis strains from South Africa and identified 79 phenolic compounds. Of these, 25 had never before been reported in Cannabis. Among them were 16 compounds tentatively classified as flavoalkaloids, a group that is rarely found in nature.

Interestingly, these flavoalkaloids were concentrated mainly in the leaves of just one of the strains, highlighting how much chemical variation can exist between different types of Cannabis. The findings were recently published in the Journal of Chromatography A.

Why These Compounds Are Hard To Detect

Dr. Magriet Muller, an analytical chemist in the LC-MS laboratory of the Central Analytical Facility (CAF) at Stellenbosch University and the study's first author, explains that studying plant phenolics is especially difficult because they occur in very small amounts and have highly diverse structures.

"Most plants contain highly complex mixtures of phenolic compounds, and while flavonoids occur widely in the plant kingdom, the flavoalkaloids are very rare in nature," she explains.

She also notes just how chemically complex Cannabis is. "We know that Cannabis is extremely complex -- it contains more than 750 metabolites -- but we did not expect such high variation in phenolic profiles between only three strains, nor to detect so many compounds for the first time in the species. Especially the first evidence of flavoalkaloids in Cannabis was very exciting."

Advanced Techniques Reveal Hidden Chemistry

As part of her postgraduate work in SU's Department of Chemistry and Polymer Science, Muller developed advanced analytical methods that combine comprehensive two-dimensional liquid chromatography with high-resolution mass spectrometry. These tools allow scientists to separate and identify compounds in extraordinary detail.

"We were looking for a new application for the methods that I developed, after successfully testing them on rooibos tea, grapes and wine. I then decided to apply the methods to Cannabis because I knew it was a complex sample, and that Cannabis phenolics have not been well characterized," she explains.

Prof. André de Villiers, who led the study and heads the analytical chemistry research group at SU, said the results were striking. "The excellent performance of two-dimensional liquid chromatography allowed separation of the flavoalkaloids from the much more abundant flavonoids, which is why we were able to detect these rare compounds for the first time in Cannabis."

Untapped Medical Potential in Overlooked Plant Material

According to Prof. de Villiers, the discovery underscores how much remains to be learned about Cannabis. So far, most research has focused on cannabinoids, the compounds responsible for the plant's psychoactive effects.

"Our analysis again highlights the medicinal potential of Cannabis plant material, currently regarded as waste. Cannabis exhibits a rich and unique non-cannabinoid phenolic profile, which could be relevant from a biomedical research perspective," he concludes.

The findings suggest that even parts of the plant often discarded, such as leaves, may hold valuable compounds with potential uses in medicine.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of rare compounds with potential medicinal benefits in cannabis leaves, a plant material often discarded. This breakthrough expands understanding of cannabis chemistry beyond cannabinoids and suggests new avenues for biomedical research with broad health implications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find rare medicinal compounds in cannabis leaves
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied cannabis mainly for cannabinoids, the chemicals that change the mind. However, other parts of the plant, like its leaves, have been less explored even though they might contain useful substances.

**What happened** – Researchers at Stellenbosch University in South Africa used advanced chemical tests to study three types of cannabis plants. They discovered 79 different phenolic compounds, which are chemicals plants make that can affect health. Among these, 25 were never seen before in cannabis. The most surprising find was a rare group called flavoalkaloids, found mainly in the leaves of one plant type.

**Impact** – This is the first time flavoalkaloids have been found in cannabis. These compounds are known for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects in other plants but are very rare in nature. Finding them in cannabis leaves shows that parts of the plant often thrown away might actually be valuable for medicine.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely study these new compounds more closely to understand how they work and if they can be used in treatments. This discovery encourages looking beyond well-known cannabis chemicals to find new medicines.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers discovered rare and potentially helpful compounds in cannabis leaves, opening new paths for medical research beyond the usual focus on cannabinoids.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des composés médicinaux rares dans les feuilles de cannabis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont surtout étudié le cannabis pour ses cannabinoïdes, les substances qui modifient l’esprit. Pourtant, d’autres parties de la plante, comme les feuilles, ont été moins explorées alors qu’elles pourraient contenir des substances utiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Stellenbosch en Afrique du Sud ont utilisé des tests chimiques avancés pour étudier trois types de cannabis. Ils ont découvert 79 composés phénoliques différents, des substances produites par les plantes qui peuvent influencer la santé. Parmi eux, 25 n’avaient jamais été vus dans le cannabis. La découverte la plus surprenante est un groupe rare appelé flavoalkaloïdes, trouvé surtout dans les feuilles d’un type de plante.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des flavoalkaloïdes sont trouvés dans le cannabis. Ces composés sont connus pour leurs effets antioxydants et anti-inflammatoires dans d’autres plantes, mais ils sont très rares dans la nature. Les trouver dans les feuilles de cannabis montre que des parties souvent jetées peuvent être précieuses pour la médecine.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement étudier ces nouveaux composés pour comprendre leur fonctionnement et leur potentiel thérapeutique. Cette découverte encourage à regarder au-delà des cannabinoïdes connus pour trouver de nouveaux médicaments.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des chercheurs ont découvert des composés rares et potentiellement utiles dans les feuilles de cannabis, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour la recherche médicale au-delà des cannabinoïdes habituels.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran compuestos medicinales raros en las hojas de cannabis
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado principalmente el cannabis por sus cannabinoides, las sustancias que afectan la mente. Sin embargo, otras partes de la planta, como las hojas, han sido menos exploradas aunque podrían contener sustancias útiles.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Stellenbosch en Sudáfrica usaron pruebas químicas avanzadas para analizar tres tipos de cannabis. Descubrieron 79 compuestos fenólicos diferentes, químicos que las plantas producen y que pueden influir en la salud. De estos, 25 nunca se habían visto antes en cannabis. Lo más sorprendente fue encontrar un grupo raro llamado flavoalcaloides, principalmente en las hojas de un tipo de planta.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se encuentran flavoalcaloides en cannabis. Estos compuestos son conocidos por sus efectos antioxidantes y antiinflamatorios en otras plantas, pero son muy raros en la naturaleza. Encontrarlos en las hojas muestra que partes de la planta que suelen desecharse pueden ser valiosas para la medicina.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente estudiarán estos nuevos compuestos para entender cómo funcionan y si pueden usarse en tratamientos. Este hallazgo anima a explorar más allá de los cannabinoides conocidos para descubrir nuevos medicamentos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Investigadores descubrieron compuestos raros y potencialmente útiles en las hojas de cannabis, abriendo nuevas vías para la investigación médica más allá de los cannabinoides habituales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of vibrant cannabis leaves illuminated softly from within, revealing intricate glowing molecular structures resembling rare flavoalkaloid compounds delicately intertwined with the leaf veins, set against a simple natural background of muted earth tones to emphasize the hidden medicinal potential within overlooked plant material.
